content:
First, we observe that whenever two domino‐tiles are not allowed to share a side, each placed domino “forbids’’ its two covered cells plus the (up to) six cells edge–adjacent to those to be used by any other domino.  Hence each domino uses up at least 8 distinct board‐cells (2 for itself, 6 in its  edge–neighborhood).  Since there are $10^6$ cells in all,

$$8\cdot(\text{number of dominoes})\;\le\;10^6
\;\Longrightarrow\;
\text{number of dominoes}\;\le\;\frac{10^6}{8}=125000.$$

On the other hand one easily attains $125000$ by the following “2×2–block’’ construction.  Break the $1000\times1000$ board into disjoint $2\times2$ blocks.  Color these blocks in a checkerboard fashion.  There are exactly $500\times500=250000$ such blocks, half of them “black’’ and half “white.’’  In each black $2\times2$–block place exactly one domino (say horizontally).  Since no two black blocks share an edge, no two of our dominoes ever become edge–adjacent (they only meet at most at a corner, which is allowed).  This yields

$$\#\{\text{dominoes}\} \;=\; \frac{250000}{2}\;=\;125000$$

exactly.  Hence the maximum possible is

\[
\boxed{125000}.
\]

On the other hand one easily attains $125000$ by the following “2×2–block’’ construction.  Break the $1000\times1000$ board into disjoint $2\times2$ blocks. There are exactly $500\times500=250000$ such blocks, half of them “black’’ and half “white.’’  In each black $2\times2$–block place exactly one domino (say horizontally). \[
